training.title: Prompt Engineering Basics for Tech Consultants

intake_summary.audience: Junior to mid-level tech consultants with no prior AI experience
intake_summary.primary_objective: Participants should understand the basic concept of prompt engineering and use a handful of broad techniques to improve generative AI output.

research_evidence.source_title: OpenAI Help Center: How to prompt ChatGPT
research_evidence.key_takeaway: Effective prompts benefit from clear instructions, examples, desired output length or format, and explicit guidance on what to do instead of only what to avoid.
research_evidence.used_for: Theory block techniques and the weak-versus-strong prompt comparison.

research_evidence.key_takeaway: For complex tasks, prompts are improved by decomposing the task, supplying relevant context, giving models time or structure to reason, and testing changes systematically.
research_evidence.used_for: Sequencing from concept to structured prompt patterns and reliability scoring for advanced techniques.

research_evidence.key_takeaway: Prompt engineering is the practice of designing inputs that help generative AI systems produce more relevant and useful outputs for the user's task.
research_evidence.used_for: Definition and framing for beginners in the kick-off and theory blocks.

research_evidence.key_takeaway: Common practical techniques include assigning a role, providing task instructions, adding context, using examples, and specifying response format.
research_evidence.used_for: Technique list, consultant scenario examples, and group exercise checklist.

programme_learning_outcomes: Explain prompt engineering as a practical method for improving generative AI outputs.
Diagnose why a vague prompt produces weak, generic, or unusable output.
Improve a prompt by adding task, context, role, constraints, examples, and output format.
Evaluate generated output against the intended consulting use case and iterate the prompt.

brief_for_trainer.session_purpose: Give consultants a practical first toolkit for using prompt engineering to get more useful AI-generated outputs in consulting tasks.
learning_outcomes: Participants can describe what prompt engineering changes in an AI interaction.
Participants can identify missing task, context, constraint, or format information in a weak prompt.
Participants can rewrite a weak consulting prompt using several broad prompt-improvement techniques.
Participants can compare two generated outputs and decide which prompt change likely improved the result.

pre_bite.purpose: Prime participants to notice how prompt specificity changes output quality.
pre_bite.participant_task: Before the session, write down one work task where you might ask a generative AI assistant for help and note what a good answer would need to include.

resources.title: OpenAI Help Center: How to prompt ChatGPT
resources.why_it_is_included: Provides accessible beginner guidance on clear instructions, examples, and output formats.

didactic_arc.learning_purpose: Create relevance and define what participants will be able to do in one hour.
trainer_actions: State the session objective in practical consulting language.
Ask participants to name one task from their pre-bite where output quality matters.
Surface the difference between using AI as a search box and using it as a task partner with instructions.
participant_actions: Share one candidate work task with a partner or table group.
Identify what would make an AI-generated answer usable for that task.
content_outline: Prompt engineering is about shaping instructions and context to improve usefulness, relevance, and format.
The session focuses on broad reusable techniques rather than one specific AI tool.
The output of the session is a rewritten prompt and a simple evaluation habit.
didactic_arc.learning_check: Participants can give one sentence on why a vague prompt creates risk in consulting work.

didactic_arc.learning_purpose: Teach the core concepts and techniques needed to improve basic prompts.
trainer_actions: Define prompt engineering and relate it to instructions, context, constraints, and evaluation.
Introduce a simple prompt structure: task, role, context, constraints, output format, examples, and success criteria.
Explain why models often need task boundaries, relevant background, and a clear target format.
Discuss common beginner mistakes: vague verbs, hidden assumptions, missing audience, missing format, and accepting the first answer uncritically.
participant_actions: Map each prompt component to a consulting deliverable such as an email, analysis summary, meeting agenda, or requirements draft.
Mark which components are missing from a short weak prompt shown by the trainer.
content_outline: Technique 1: clarify the task and audience.
Technique 2: add relevant context without dumping irrelevant information.
Technique 3: set role, constraints, and success criteria.
Technique 4: specify output format and level of detail.
Technique 5: provide examples or patterns when the target style is hard to infer.
Technique 6: iterate and evaluate the output instead of treating the first response as final.
didactic_arc.learning_check: Participants label missing prompt components in a weak prompt and explain the likely output problem.

didactic_arc.learning_purpose: Make the theory concrete through a realistic consulting prompt transformation.
trainer_actions: Show a weak prompt for a consultant task, such as drafting client discovery questions for a data platform migration.
Walk through how to add task, audience, context, constraints, output format, and evaluation criteria.
Compare expected differences between the weak and improved prompt outputs.
participant_actions: Identify which prompt additions are most likely to improve relevance.
Predict what failure mode remains even after the prompt is improved.
content_outline: Weak prompt: ask for generic discovery questions with no client context or output format.
Improved prompt: specify consultant role, client situation, stakeholder audience, constraints, desired categories, and concise output format.
Evaluation habit: check whether the answer fits the task, audience, constraints, and next action.
didactic_arc.learning_check: Participants name two concrete changes that make the improved prompt more useful than the weak version.

didactic_arc.learning_purpose: Have participants actively apply the prompt-improvement techniques to a work-relevant scenario.
trainer_actions: Distribute the group exercise sheet and assign small groups.
Ask groups to diagnose the weak prompt before rewriting it.
Facilitate a short comparison of rewritten prompts and ask groups to explain their design choices.
Debrief using the prompt component checklist.
participant_actions: Use the exercise sheet to identify missing prompt components.
Rewrite the prompt using at least four broad techniques from the theory block.
State how the group would evaluate whether the generated output is good enough for client work.
content_outline: Group task: improve a vague consulting prompt into a structured prompt.
Required output: rewritten prompt plus a short evaluation checklist.
Debrief focus: which added context matters, which constraints reduce ambiguity, and what still requires human judgement.
didactic_arc.learning_check: Each group produces a rewritten prompt that includes task, context, constraints, output format, and evaluation criteria.

didactic_arc.learning_purpose: Consolidate the prompt-engineering toolkit and connect it to post-session application.
trainer_actions: Ask participants to choose one prompt technique they will use first in client or internal work.
participant_actions: Write one personal action for applying the prompt checklist.
Note one type of AI output that should always be reviewed by a human before client use.
content_outline: Prompt engineering is an iterative workflow: instruct, contextualize, constrain, format, test, and refine.
Better prompts reduce ambiguity but do not remove responsibility for human review.
The post-bite transfers the method to a real participant task.
didactic_arc.learning_check: Participants can name the six-part prompt checklist and one review habit.

deck_outline.title: Why Prompt Engineering Matters
deck_outline.learning_purpose: Anchor the session in consultant work where output quality affects usefulness.
deck_outline.key_message: A prompt is not just a question; it is a work instruction for producing a useful output.
suggested_content: Session objective and expected participant output
Examples of consultant tasks where better prompts matter
Contrast between vague request and purposeful instruction

reliability.rationale: The framing is grounded in official prompt guidance and directly matches the user's stated audience and objective.

deck_outline.title: The One-Hour Goal
deck_outline.learning_purpose: Set clear expectations for what participants will learn and practice.
deck_outline.key_message: By the end, participants will diagnose weak prompts, rewrite them, and evaluate the result.

reliability.rationale: This content is based on the validated intake and Maverx didactic structure.

deck_outline.title: What Prompt Engineering Is
deck_outline.learning_purpose: Give beginners a practical definition without tool-specific detail.
deck_outline.key_message: Prompt engineering is designing instructions and context so a generative AI system can produce a more relevant output.
suggested_content: Relationship between prompt, model response, and human review
Why vague prompts produce generic answers

reliability.rationale: The definition is supported by official OpenAI and Google Cloud guidance and is stable at beginner level.

deck_outline.title: The Prompt Checklist
deck_outline.learning_purpose: Introduce a reusable structure participants can apply immediately.
deck_outline.key_message: Strong prompts usually specify task, role, context, constraints, output format, examples, and success criteria.

reliability.rationale: The checklist synthesizes recurring recommendations across official OpenAI, Microsoft, and Google sources.

deck_outline.title: Common Beginner Failure Modes
deck_outline.learning_purpose: Help participants diagnose why a prompt underperforms.
deck_outline.key_message: Weak prompts often hide the real task, audience, constraints, or quality criteria.
suggested_content: No target audience or level of detail
No required format or review criteria

reliability.rationale: The failure modes are a practical synthesis of prompt guidance and consulting experience, so trainer calibration is useful.

deck_outline.title: Worked Example: Discovery Questions
deck_outline.learning_purpose: Show how the checklist changes a realistic consulting prompt.
deck_outline.key_message: Adding context, constraints, and output format turns a generic request into a task-ready prompt.
suggested_content: Weak prompt for client discovery questions
Improved prompt with role, context, constraints, and output format
Expected improvement in relevance and usability

reliability.rationale: The example is realistic for tech consulting but should be adapted to the trainer's client context.

deck_outline.title: Group Exercise: Rewrite the Prompt
deck_outline.learning_purpose: Move participants from recognition to active prompt improvement.
deck_outline.key_message: The technique becomes useful when participants can apply it to a messy, underspecified work task.
suggested_content: Evaluation question for the generated output

reliability.rationale: The exercise directly reinforces the theory block and follows established active-learning practice.

deck_outline.title: Debrief: What Changed the Output
deck_outline.learning_purpose: Help participants connect prompt edits to likely output improvements.
deck_outline.key_message: Good prompt edits are intentional: each added instruction should reduce ambiguity or improve evaluation.
suggested_content: Discuss remaining risks and need for human review

reliability.rationale: The debrief logic is grounded in prompt iteration guidance, but exact discussion quality depends on participant outputs.

deck_outline.title: Use the Checklist, Then Review
deck_outline.learning_purpose: Consolidate the toolkit and reinforce responsible use.
deck_outline.key_message: Prompt engineering improves output quality, but consultants still need to review accuracy, relevance, and client fit.
suggested_content: One review habit before using AI output in work

reliability.rationale: The recap is based on official prompting guidance and a stable human-review principle for consulting work.

handout.purpose: Guide small groups through diagnosing and improving a weak consulting prompt.

instructions: Read the weak prompt and underline missing task, context, constraint, format, or success-criteria information.
Rewrite the prompt so it includes at least four checklist components.
Add a two- or three-point evaluation checklist for deciding whether the generated output is usable.
Prepare to explain which prompt addition would most improve the output.
activities.participant_output: A rewritten prompt and a short output evaluation checklist.

post_bite.purpose: Transfer the prompt-engineering method to a real participant work task.
post_bite.participant_task: Take the work task you identified before the session, write a structured prompt using the checklist, and note one change you would make after reviewing the first generated answer.

resources.title: OpenAI Platform Documentation: Prompt engineering
resources.why_it_is_included: Provides additional official guidance for participants who want to deepen their prompting practice after the session.

issues: Trainer should adapt the worked example to the participants' consulting domain if a more specific client context is known.
